semesters.focus: Programming Foundations + DSA Basics + Git + Web Introduction
semesters.objective: By the end of II‑I, every student can write basic programs in Python/Java/C, understand fundamental DSA, use Git/GitHub, and have a taste of HTML/CSS/JS.

topics.text: Syntax, variables, conditionals, loops, functions, I/O.
Basic OOP: classes, objects, methods, constructors.

modules.brief: Fundamentals of programming in Python including syntax, conditionals, functions, object-oriented concepts, and debugging.

topics.text: Time and space complexity intuition (Big‑O at a high level).
Simple problems from platforms (2 h of guided practice).

modules.brief: Introduction to basic data structures (arrays, strings), recursion ideas, and Big-O runtime analysis.

topics.text: Git basics: init, add, commit, log, branch, merge.
Remote repositories: clone, push, pull, pull request workflow.
Classroom activity: students push a small programming assignment repo.

modules.brief: Version control essentials with Git, remote repositories on GitHub, and pull request workflows.

topics.text: HTML fundamentals: structure, forms, common tags.
CSS essentials: selectors, box model, basic layout.
JavaScript intro: variables, functions, DOM basics, events.

modules.brief: Foundational web skills: HTML5 markup structure, CSS selectors/layouts, and basic JavaScript page interactivity.

topics.text: SDLC models; emphasis on Agile/Scrum basics (roles, ceremonies, user stories).
Versioning + issue tracking (GitHub Issues, basic Kanban).

modules.brief: Software development lifecycles, Agile/Scrum framework roles, issue tracking, and versioning standards.

semesters.post_assessment: Baseline coding test to decide who leans to SE/FSD vs Data vs Cloud/DevOps/Security.

pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable solving problems using basic DSA (arrays, strings, simple linked
lists/stacks/queues, basic recursion and complexity) in at least one programming language
(C++/Java/Python), with prior exposure to introductory OOP, relational databases/SQL, and a
simple web/console app deployed once, so this semester can focus on strengthening patterns,
applying OOP/system design, working with transactions and simple cloud CI/CD.

modules.module_title: Advanced DSA & Problem Solving – 20 h (Core SE)

details.title: Trees/BSTs, heaps, graphs at intro–intermediate level.
Classic patterns (sliding window, two pointers, binary search) on easy–medium problems.
Guided sets on easy–medium questions with ample scaffolding.
Emphasis on writing clear, correct solutions and explaining logic.
Use AI mainly to:

details.text: Re‑explain problems, suggest pseudo‑code, generate additional test cases.

details.title: Strong emphasis: students must first attempt manually, then compare with AI and discuss complexity/edge cases. Focus: Confidence and correctness on core patterns + healthy AI usage habits.

modules.brief: Deep dive into advanced data structures and algorithm analysis with hands-on competitive programming practice.

modules.module_title: System Design & OOP in Practice – 10 h (Core SE)

details.title: Simple examples, step‑by‑step UML for small systems (Library Management, etc.).
1–2 small case studies, focusing on correct decomposition, not scale.

topics.title: AI in Design & Code Review – 1 h

details.title: Use AI review tools to check style, naming, basic refactors.
Students practice accepting/rejecting suggestions thoughtfully. Focus: Solid fundamentals, no pressure to design very complex systems yet.

modules.brief: Applying SOLID design principles and standard software design patterns to architect robust systems.

modules.module_title: Databases & Transactions for SE – 8 h (Core SE)

details.title: Clean schema for simple apps, focus on 3NF and basic indexing.
Simple transactions and stored procedures.

Show how AI suggests more efficient queries; students compare EXPLAIN output and indexes conceptually. Focus: Comfort with relational modeling and safe, readable SQL, plus awareness of AI tuning tools.

modules.brief: Deep dive into relational database design, query optimization, ACID transactions, and indexing structures.

details.title: Deploy a small app to a single VM, set up logs.
Very simple pipeline (build + unit tests + deploy).

Explain use‑cases (AI scanning logs, flagging failing patterns) conceptually. Focus: They can deploy and interpret a simple pipeline, and understand where AI plugs in, but not necessarily configure advanced tooling.

modules.brief: Overview of containerization (Docker), continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), and cloud-native hosting.

pre_requisites: Students should be strong at solving medium‑level DSA problems (arrays, strings, linked lists,
trees/BSTs, graphs, recursion/DP basics, standard patterns with time‑complexity reasoning) in at
least one language (C++/Java/Python), with solid OOP and low‑level design exposure, comfort
designing and querying relational schemas, and prior experience deploying a simple app with a
basic CI/CD pipeline, so this track can focus on interview‑grade DSA, richer system design,
performance‑aware SQL, and more realistic cloud/AI‑augmented pipelines.

modules.module_title: Advanced DSA & Problem Solving – 20 h (Advanced SE)

details.title: Same content as Core, but with more emphasis on tricky use‑cases:

details.text: Trees: LCA, diameter, tree DP (basic forms).
Graphs: more varied problems (connected components, simple shortest paths).
Patterns: multi‑source BFS, binary search on answer, prefix sums, etc.

details.title: Medium difficulty problems (and a few gateway hard ones) on coding platforms.
Timed practice and editorial reading, post‑analysis of failed attempts.
Use AI as coach:

details.text: Ask for constraints analysis, alternative solutions, edge‑case generation.
Use AI to get feedback on complexity and code quality after they submit their own solution.

details.title: Discuss ethics around AI use in contests/interviews, aligning with platform rules. Focus: Interview‑grade DSA strength plus disciplined AI use tailored to high‑performer expectations.

modules.brief: Deep dive into advanced data structures and algorithm analysis with hands-on competitive programming practice.

modules.module_title: System Design & OOP in Practice – 10 h (Advanced SE)

details.title: More complex scenarios: multi‑module systems, more entities, constraints.
Case studies with more components: simple feed system, simplified booking system, etc.

topics.title: AI in Design & Code Review – 1 h (Deeper)

details.title: Use AI tools to critique and refine their designs (e.g., identify code smells, security issues, possible microservice boundaries).

Compare AI’s suggestions with students’ own design choices and industry best practices. Focus: Preparing for LLD/System Design rounds at better companies, where AI is part of code review and design tooling.

modules.brief: Applying SOLID design principles and standard software design patterns to architect robust systems.

modules.module_title: Databases & Transactions for SE – 8 h (Advanced SE)

details.title: Design schemas for slightly larger systems (multi‑tenant or with reporting tables).
Query optimization scenarios (indexes, query patterns).
More involved transactions (e.g., multi‑step operations that need rollback handling).
Give a slow query, use AI to propose variants, and validate with EXPLAIN plans or timing in DB. Focus: Building capacity to reason about performance, not just write correct SQL.

modules.brief: Deep dive into relational database design, query optimization, ACID transactions, and indexing structures.

details.title: Same VM deployment, plus:

details.text: Basic security (security groups, minimal IAM role concept).
Simple horizontal scaling idea or using a PaaS option.

details.title: Pipeline with linting + unit tests + simple security check (SAST or dependency scan).

topics.title: AI in CI/CD & Monitoring – 2 h (More Applied)

details.title: Discuss scenarios where:

details.text: AI analyzes build/test logs to flag flaky tests, risky changes.

AI tools act as a “quality gate” in the pipeline; students design an example stage in YAML/diagram form. Focus: High performers see how to design pipelines that could integrate AI‑based checks and evaluate risk, closer to top product companies’ practices.

modules.brief: Overview of containerization (Docker), continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), and cloud-native hosting.

sub_tracks.sub_track_name: Track A – Core Deep Tech (Average Performers)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable solving basic to lower‑medium DSA problems (arrays, strings,
simple linked lists/stacks/queues, introductory trees/graphs, recursion, and Big‑O reasoning) in at
least one programming language (C++/Java/Python), with prior exposure to OOP fundamentals,
simple relational schemas/SQL, and having seen at least one basic web/console app deployed, so
this semester can focus on strengthening patterns, applying OOP/system design, working with
transactions, and using simple cloud + CI/CD flows with AI assistance.

details.title: Trees & BST (traversal, basic insert/delete, simple path questions) at intro–intermediate level.
Heaps & Priority Queues (standard problems: k‑largest, scheduling).
Graphs: adjacency list, BFS/DFS, simple shortest path examples (unweighted or basic weighted).
Classic patterns: sliding window, two pointers, standard binary search, one or two “binary search on answer” examples.
Guided practice on easy–medium problems.
Faculty provides stepwise hints; focus is on attaining correctness and clarity.

topics.title: AI‑Assisted Coding – Good & Bad Usage – 2 h

details.title: AI used mainly as a teacher:
Explaining problem statements and constraints in simpler words.
Suggesting pseudo‑code or test cases after a student attempts on their own.
Discussion:
Risks of copy‑pasting full solutions.

Need to manually check complexity and edge cases even when AI writes code. Expected Outcome (Core): Students can reliably solve easy to lower‑medium DSA problems independently, and use AI as a tutor to clarify concepts and generate test cases, not as an answer engine.

modules.brief: Deep dive into advanced data structures and algorithm analysis with hands-on competitive programming practice.

modules.module_title: System Design & OOP in Practice – 10 h (Core)

details.title: Reinforce basic OOP: encapsulation, inheritance, composition.
Apply SOLID principles to small, single‑service examples.
UML basics: small class diagrams and straightforward sequence diagrams.
Case studies such as Library Management, simple Task Manager.
Focus on clear class design and relationships, not scale or performance.

topics.title: AI in Design & Code Review – 1 h

details.title: Use AI code review tools to:
Spot simple style issues, minor code smells
Suggest refactors for small methods.

Students practice accepting/rejecting suggestions and explaining why. Expected Outcome (Core): Students can design and implement well‑structured small systems, and understand how AI code review assists in basic refactoring and style checks without replacing human judgment.

modules.brief: Applying SOLID design principles and standard software design patterns to architect robust systems.

details.title: Design simple schemas (e.g., Library, Task Manager) up to 3NF.
Write essential queries with joins and basic aggregates.
ACID concepts explained via simple examples.
One or two basic stored procedures/functions.
Show a non‑optimal query and an AI‑suggested improvement.

Discuss why the improved query is better (index use, filter order), at an intuitive level. Expected Outcome (Core): Students can model and query standard relational problems, and appreciate how AI tools can suggest better SQL, while still needing to know the relational concepts themselves.

modules.brief: Deep dive into relational database design, query optimization, ACID transactions, and indexing structures.

topics.title: Cloud Basics for SE & Simple Deployment – 5 h

details.title: Deploy a simple monolithic web/service app to a single cloud VM.
Introduce logs and basic monitoring (viewing logs, basic metrics dashboards).
Single pipeline: build → run tests → deploy.
Basic configuration using GitHub Actions/Jenkins/GitLab CI.

topics.title: AI in CI/CD & Production Monitoring – 2 h (Awareness)

details.title: Conceptual:
AI watches build/test logs to detect flaky tests or unusual failures.

AI assists in release decisions based on past failure patterns and metrics. Expected Outcome (Core): Students can deploy and maintain a basic cloud app with a simple CI/CD pipeline, and understand at a high level how AI tools augment observability and reliability.

modules.brief: Overview of containerization (Docker), continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), and cloud-native hosting.

sub_tracks.sub_track_name: Track B – Advanced Deep Tech (High Performers)
pre_requisites: Students should be strong at solving medium‑level DSA problems (arrays, strings, linked lists,
trees/BSTs, graphs, recursion/DP basics, standard patterns with time‑/space‑complexity analysis)
in at least one language (C++/Java/Python), with solid prior exposure to OOP and low‑level
design, relational schema design and SQL, and deploying a simple app with a basic CI/CD
pipeline, so this track can focus on interview‑grade DSA, richer system design,
performance‑aware databases, and more realistic cloud/AI‑augmented DevOps workflows.

details.title: Same topics, but with richer cases:
Trees: LCA, diameter, simple tree DP forms.
Graphs: multiple BFS/DFS‑based problems, basic shortest path in weighted graphs.
Patterns: advanced sliding window, prefix sums, more “binary search on answer” tasks.
Problem sets mostly in medium range and some gateway hard problems.
Timed sessions; include editorial reading & post‑mortems as part of learning.
Use AI as a coach:
Ask for alternate approaches and complexity analysis after writing own solutions.
Generate corner‑case tests and compare performance.

Discussion on ethical use during interviews (strict no‑use in live rounds). Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students can handle medium DSA interview problems confidently, and use AI strategically for reflection, optimization ideas, and advanced test generation.

modules.brief: Deep dive into advanced data structures and algorithm analysis with hands-on competitive programming practice.

modules.module_title: System Design & OOP in Practice – 10 h (Advanced)

details.title: More complex case studies (e.g., Ride‑sharing, simplified booking or feed system).
Introduce trade‑offs (flexibility vs complexity, over‑engineering vs under‑design).
Students produce more detailed UML (classes, key interactions, basic layering).

topics.title: AI in Design & Code Review – 1 h (Deeper)

details.title: Use AI tools to:
Identify potential security issues, performance hotspots, and deeper code smells.

Suggest alternative designs (e.g., layering, patterns) and discuss trade‑offs. Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students are better prepared for LLD/system‑design style interviews, understanding how AI can assist in design critique while they remain the decision‑makers.

modules.brief: Applying SOLID design principles and standard software design patterns to architect robust systems.

details.title: Schemas for slightly larger systems (e.g., multi‑user, simple analytics requirements).
Queries involving non‑trivial joins and basic performance considerations.
Transactions spanning multiple tables; error handling patterns.

Students experiment with AI‑suggested query rewrites and compare actual run‑time/EXPLAIN plans (guided by faculty). Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students can reason about database performance and schema choices, and use AI tools to help tune performance, not just write queries.

modules.brief: Deep dive into relational database design, query optimization, ACID transactions, and indexing structures.

topics.title: Cloud Basics for SE & Deployment – 5 h

details.title: Same fundamentals as Core plus:
Exposure to basic PaaS (e.g., managed app service) or one extra environment.
Simple hardening: security groups, least‑privilege IAM role (concept).
Pipeline includes linting + tests + a simple security or quality check (e.g., dependency vulnerability scan).
Students design (even at a conceptual/YAML level) a pipeline stage where an AI “quality gate” could run:

E.g., AI analyzes code changes + test results to flag risky deployments. Expected Outcome (Advanced): High performers can design and implement more realistic DevOps workflows, and can articulate where and how AI can be plugged into their pipelines for smarter decisions.

modules.brief: Overview of containerization (Docker), continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), and cloud-native hosting.

sub_tracks.sub_track_name: Track A – Core Advanced Topics (Average Performers)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable solving easy–medium DSA problems (arrays, strings, basic
linked lists/stacks/queues, introductory trees/graphs, recursion and time‑complexity reasoning) in
at least one programming language (C++/Java/Python), with prior experience building a small
app using OOP and a relational DB (CRUD + simple feature) and deploying it once to a basic
cloud setup/CI pipeline, so this semester can focus on interview‑oriented DSA practice,
conceptual concurrency/microservices, and an end‑to‑end mini project with honest AI‑assisted
development.

details.title: Problem sets: easy–medium, mostly medium‑ish on arrays, strings, trees, graphs, DP basics, common patterns.
Emphasis on:
Clear problem breakdown and brute‑force first.
Time/space complexity reasoning for their final solution.
Explicit practice listing edge cases before coding.
Simulated 1:1 rounds with moderate‑difficulty questions.
Strong focus on:
Thinking aloud, clarifying requirements.
Explaining trade‑offs and next steps, even if solution is not fully optimal.

topics.title: Using AI Tools Ethically for Interview Prep – 4 h (Core)

details.title: Landscape: copilot tools, AI interview assistants, AI‑driven practice platforms, but positioned as support.
Do’s for Core track:
Use AI to get hints, pattern recognition help, or summary of editorial after attempting.
Use AI to generate extra test cases and verify solutions.
Don’ts:
Copy‑pasting entire solutions; using AI during actual interviews/assessments.
Guided activity (simpler variant):
Students solve a medium problem.
Then use an AI assistant to:

details.text: Identify one or two improvements (better variable names, slightly better complexity if available).

Document what they learned from the comparison. Expected Outcome (Core): Students become comfortable with standard interview‑level (easy–medium) questions, and learn to use AI tools as practice partners that give hints, explanations, and tests, without compromising integrity.

modules.brief: Intensive problem-solving sessions on key DSA concepts, interview patterns, and LeetCode-style challenges.

details.title: Threads vs processes; shared memory; conceptual understanding.
Synchronization: locks, race conditions, deadlocks at a diagram + pseudo‑code level.
Simple examples (producer–consumer, shared counter) discussed more than implemented.
Monolith vs microservices, small examples.
REST API best practices: resource naming, status codes, idempotency.
Observability basics (logs, metrics, traces) explained using simple sample logs and diagrams.

topics.title: AI in Production Systems (Debugging & Monitoring) – 2 h (Core)

details.title: Conceptual: AI agents/analytics that:
Scan logs/traces for latency spikes, error surges.
Raise alerts or basic suggestions.

Emphasis that good logging and clear architecture from developers makes AI‑driven observability effective. Expected Outcome (Core): Students understand concurrency and microservices at a conceptual and small‑scale example level, and see how AI‑powered observability tools help monitor/debug systems, provided fundamentals (logging, error handling) are sound.

modules.brief: Advanced software development topics including multi-threading, concurrency control, caching strategies, and system optimization.

details.title: End‑to‑end console or web application in Java/Python, with:
Normalized relational DB schema.
CRUD + one meaningful feature (search/filter/simple analytics).
Deployed to a cloud VM or basic PaaS.
Students must document:
Where they used AI (boilerplate, tests, simple refactors).
How they checked correctness of AI‑generated code (manual tests, reviews).
Allowed but not required to fully implement.
At minimum, they can design (in documentation) an AI/LLM API integration (e.g., chatbot/help, simple suggestion) with request/response schema.
Add basic logs and a few simple metrics.

In documentation, describe high‑level how an AI monitoring tool could use these to detect unusual error spikes or frequent failed operations. Expected Outcome (Core): Students deliver a clean, working project, and honestly reflect on where AI helped and how they validated it, plus a basic understanding of how AI‑based monitoring would sit around such a system.

modules.brief: Applying semester concepts to build, document, and present a functional technical project prototype.

pre_requisites: Students should be strong at solving medium‑level DSA problems (arrays, strings, linked lists,
trees/BSTs, graphs, recursion/DP basics, pattern‑based problem solving with clear time/space
analysis) in at least one language (C++/Java/Python), with solid prior experience in OOP and
low‑level design, relational schema design and SQL, and deploying a DB‑backed app with a
simple CI/CD pipeline, so this track can focus on company‑level competitive programming,
deeper SE topics (concurrency, microservices), and production‑like AI‑augmented projects.

details.title: Problem sets: mostly medium, some medium‑hard in arrays, strings, trees, graphs, DP basics, patterns.
Pattern‑driven practice: explicitly mapping problems to known patterns (two pointers, union‑find, BFS/DFS, DP on trees), in line with pattern‑based interview prep advice.
Strong emphasis on:
Fast pattern recognition.
Deriving and arguing complexity before coding.
Simulated interviews with higher difficulty and time pressure, closer to top‑product expectations.
Additional focus on:
Explaining trade‑offs between multiple approaches (e.g., brute force vs optimized).
Post‑interview self‑analysis.

topics.title: Using AI Tools Ethically for Interview Prep – 4 h (Advanced)

details.title: Same Do’s/Don’ts, but with deeper and more demanding usage:
Using AI tutors to generate personalized practice paths and targeted problems based on weaknesses.
Employing AI for real‑time code analysis and feedback after submissions (style, efficiency, edge‑case warnings).
Guided activity (richer):
Students solve a medium+ problem.
Feed their solution to an AI tool that can comment on complexity and edge cases.
They must:

details.text: Write a short critique of AI’s feedback (what was useful, what was wrong/irrelevant).

Improve their solution based on that analysis. Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students approach company‑level coding rounds with higher confidence, using AI as a coach (personalized paths, feedback on style/complexity) while strictly maintaining interview integrity.

modules.brief: Intensive problem-solving sessions on key DSA concepts, interview patterns, and LeetCode-style challenges.

details.title: Same concepts, but with more depth and code:
Write or reason about simple concurrent code samples.
Discuss real scenarios (thread pools, producer–consumer with bounded buffer).
Deeper discussion:
Simple design of a small microservice system (e.g., user service + order service).
Trade‑offs (latency vs consistency).

topics.title: AI in Production Systems (Debugging & Monitoring) – 2 h (Advanced)

details.title: Go beyond awareness:
Design a log format and tracing identifiers that would help AI tools correlate events across services.

Case analysis: given sample logs/traces from multiple services, students outline how an AI engine could infer a root cause. Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students can reason more concretely about real production issues, and intentionally structure logs and architecture so that AI‑powered observability tools can be effective.

modules.brief: Advanced software development topics including multi-threading, concurrency control, caching strategies, and system optimization.

details.title: End‑to‑end app with DB, CRUD, one meaningful feature, cloud deployment.
More demanding reflection:
Students must include examples of rejecting AI suggestions and explain why (wrong logic, too complex, insecure, etc.).
They should use AI explicitly for test generation, refactoring suggestions, and basic performance hints, then measure/validate changes.

topics.title: Optional AI Feature Integration (Advanced – strongly encouraged):

details.title: High performers are expected to at least prototype one of:
Live or stubbed AI/LLM API (chatbot, suggestion, summarization).
Or a well‑specified mock with request/response contract and UI integrated.
Add more structured logs and simple metrics (latency, error counts).

In the report, they should sketch a simple AI‑driven alerting rule or anomaly detection scenario using those logs/metrics. Expected Outcome (Advanced): Students not only build a production‑like app but also show mature, critical use of AI in the dev lifecycle, and can convincingly describe how AI‑driven monitoring would wrap around their system.

modules.brief: Applying semester concepts to build, document, and present a functional technical project prototype.
Preparing for campus placements with company-specific mock tests, technical aptitude drills, and MCQ reviews.
Improving placement readiness with professional resumes, mock interviews, and group discussion drills.
